text:
This paper deals with the exposition of the first commandment in the Heidelberg Catechism (HC). Alongside the theologically pioneering work of Philipp Melanchthon, it examines Zacharias Ursinus’ view of the Decalogue, taking into account his role as the main author of the HC. Ursinus studied under Melanchthon in Wittenberg and it is assumed here that both of them agree on a theological concept of the Decalogue. Therefore, the article makes a comparison between the German and Latin versions of the HC and Melanchthon’s Loci Communes from 1521 as well as his Heubtartikel from 1553. The work of Ursinus is brought into the discussion in the form of his preliminary drafts, the Catechesis Minor and Maior, and his later dogmatic lectures in the Corpus doctrinae christianae.

The first article explores how the Reformers understood the first commandment. An exposition of their understanding of the first commandment unfolding from exegesis of scripture to their application of it to the context of their time is undertaken. It emerges that the first commandment was not merely seen as a prohibition of literal idols, but more fundamentally as an issue of trusting, loving and reverencing the God of the exodus and the resurrection – the trinitarian God. A final section reflects on the relevance of the Reformers’ perspectives for South Africa in the context of a pluralistic society where the Reformers’ discussion of magic, superstition and the exclusive claim of the gospel of Jesus Christ may provide a theological framework for engaging contemporary issues such as occultism and existential anxiety in Africa.
